A sustained higher PA diastolic mean may reflect elevated filling pressures/venous congestion, which can worsen renal function. However, mean aggregation is less specific than extremes for AKI-relevant episodes.

Later sustained elevation in PA diastolic mean can indicate persistent congestion and impaired forward flow, both associated with cardiorenal AKI after cardiac surgery. Still indirect compared with direct renal markers.

High PA diastolic maxima can capture acute congestion or pulmonary hypertension episodes that may contribute to renal venous congestion. In the immediate post-op period, transient spikes are common, so importance is capped.

Max PA diastolic pressure during 13–24h may reflect episodic high filling pressures/congestion, which is linked to AKI via increased renal venous pressure. As an extreme it is more informative than the mean.

Late high PA diastolic maxima suggest ongoing significant congestion or pulmonary vascular load near the outcome window, supporting risk for cardiorenal AKI. Not a definitive biomarker but a meaningful hemodynamic contributor.

Low PA diastolic minima may indicate low preload/low flow states that can reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op lows are often iatrogenic or transient. As an extreme it has some signal but limited specificity.

A low PA diastolic minimum in the trajectory phase can reflect intermittent hypovolemia or low cardiac output, which can predispose to renal hypoperfusion. It remains an indirect surrogate and less specific than systemic pressures.

Low diastolic pulmonary artery pressure can reflect low right-sided filling pressures/low preload or vasodilation, contributing to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. In the 25–36h window, a sustained low extreme may signal ongoing hemodynamic vulnerability relevant to AKI risk.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity (e.g., PA catheter use) rather than a direct renal mechanism. Early post-op (0–12h) ordering is routine in higher-acuity cardiac surgery patients and is only a weak proxy for AKI risk.
Counts of PAP measurements indicate clinician concern/acuity rather than physiology. In 13–24h it may correlate with instability but remains an indirect behavioral marker.
More frequent PAP monitoring late (25–36h) can indicate persistent shock/heart failure management, which is associated with AKI risk. Still, it is a proxy for severity rather than a direct biomarker.

Average heart rate in the immediate post-op period is influenced by pain, pacing, inotropes, and transient stress responses, limiting specificity for renal hypoperfusion. Mean values also smooth over brief but important hypotensive/arrhythmic episodes.

Sustained tachycardia can reflect ongoing low output, sepsis, or bleeding, which can contribute to AKI, but mean HR is nonspecific. Extremes (max/min) generally capture clinically meaningful insults better than the mean.

Persistent tachycardia/bradycardia near the outcome window may reflect ongoing hemodynamic stress affecting renal perfusion, but mean HR remains nonspecific. To reduce collinearity, extremes are more informative than the mean for AKI-related insults.

Marked tachycardia can indicate bleeding, pain/agitation, low stroke volume, or arrhythmia, all of which may reduce effective renal perfusion. In 0–12h this is common post-op, so importance is capped and interpreted conservatively.
High peak heart rate in 13–24h may signal deterioration (ongoing shock, sepsis, atrial fibrillation with RVR) that can precipitate renal hypoperfusion. Max captures acute stress better than mean HR.

Late peak tachycardia close to hour-60 creatinine can reflect persistent low output, sepsis, or uncontrolled arrhythmia, which are strong contributors to AKI. The extreme value is a better marker of acute physiologic insult than the mean.

Bradycardia can reflect conduction disease, excessive beta-blockade/sedation, or pacing issues leading to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Early post-op bradycardia can be transient, so the score is conservative.

Low minimum heart rate in 13–24h may indicate clinically significant bradyarrhythmia or over-suppression contributing to low cardiac output and renal ischemia. Min captures potentially harmful episodes better than mean HR.

Late bradycardic episodes (min HR) can directly reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion, especially in elderly post-cardiac surgery patients with limited reserve. Proximity to the outcome makes this a stronger leading indicator.
Heart rate measurement frequency is largely routine ICU monitoring and does not represent a renal mechanism. It provides minimal incremental information for AKI prediction.
Pulse measurement counts reflect documentation/monitoring practices rather than physiology. It is unlikely to add specific prognostic value for creatinine rise.
Even late in the course, pulse measurement frequency is standard continuous monitoring and not a direct indicator of AKI pathophysiology. Any association would be incidental and weak.

Average inspired O2 reflects overall respiratory support but is not a direct AKI driver; early post-op values are often transient. Extremes (hypoxemia/very high FiO2) would be more pathophysiologically relevant than the mean.

High peak O2 requirement can proxy acute pulmonary dysfunction and systemic stress that may contribute to renal hypoxia, but it is indirect. In the immediate 0–12h window this is common post-op and thus only modestly informative.

Low minimum O2 delivery (if reflecting low FiO2 tolerated) may indicate stability, while if reflecting hypoxemia it could relate to renal ischemic risk; interpretation is indirect. Extremes are more relevant than mean, but early post-op limits strength.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. More frequent documentation may correlate with instability but is a weak proxy.
O2 measurement frequency in this epoch is still primarily a care-pattern marker rather than a physiologic AKI trigger. It may weakly reflect ongoing respiratory/hemodynamic concern.

Closer to the outcome, frequent O2 documentation may better proxy persistent critical illness and clinician concern, which correlates with AKI risk. Still not a direct physiologic driver, so kept low.
Nitrous oxide exposure has no established direct link to postoperative AKI in this context. Mean use in the immediate post-op period is unlikely to add renal prognostic value.

Peak nitrous oxide use is not a recognized trigger for renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity in typical cardiac ICU practice. Any association would be confounded by anesthesia practice rather than kidney injury biology.
Minimum nitrous oxide use is clinically unrelated to AKI risk and largely reflects anesthetic management choices. No meaningful mechanistic connection to creatinine rise.
Frequency of recording nitrous oxide is a documentation/monitoring artifact with no plausible direct relationship to AKI. At most it reflects routine anesthesia charting.
Nitrous oxide measurement frequency in this later epoch is unlikely to be clinically meaningful for renal outcomes. It reflects practice patterns rather than renal physiology.
Even near the outcome, nitrous oxide measurement frequency is not a credible proxy for impending AKI. No established linkage to creatinine trajectory.
Inspired air fraction/flow as an anesthetic carrier gas has no direct renal relevance. Mean values are dominated by ventilator/anesthesia setup rather than kidney perfusion.
Peak air delivery is not a known AKI risk factor and mainly reflects ventilator gas-mixing settings. No direct mechanistic pathway to creatinine rise.
Minimum air delivery similarly reflects ventilator/anesthesia configuration rather than patient physiology relevant to AKI. Not expected to predict postoperative creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of air settings is routine charting and not a marker of renal risk. It provides little signal beyond standard-of-care monitoring.
Measurement frequency of delivered air is a documentation/monitoring artifact and not a physiologic driver of AKI. At most it reflects routine ventilator/anesthesia charting rather than renal risk.
Counts of air administration/recording do not mechanistically relate to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. It is primarily a care-process variable, not an AKI predictor.

Isoflurane exposure can influence blood pressure, but mean dose in the immediate post-op window is an indirect and nonspecific proxy for hemodynamic management. Early (0–12h) anesthetic carryover is common and transient, limiting AKI specificity.

A high peak isoflurane level may indicate episodes of deeper anesthesia with hypotension risk, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However, it is still an indirect marker and early post-op derangements are often transient (0–12h cap).
Minimum isoflurane level is not a meaningful marker of renal insult and is largely determined by weaning/off status. It has little direct relationship to AKI pathophysiology.
Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. In this context it is unlikely to encode specific renal risk beyond routine care.
How often isoflurane is recorded is a process-of-care variable and not a direct determinant of kidney injury. It may weakly reflect acuity but is not specific for impending AKI.

Recording frequency of isoflurane does not capture renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure directly. It is primarily behavioral/monitoring related, so it should be kept low to avoid spurious associations.

Mean SvO2 reflects global oxygen delivery/consumption balance and can correlate with low cardiac output states that predispose to AKI. As an average in the immediate post-op period it is less sensitive than extremes and early instability is common (0–12h cap).

Persistently low average SvO2 in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing inadequate oxygen delivery/low cardiac output, a recognized contributor to renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Mean is still less specific than the minimum but can reflect sustained shock physiology.

High SvO2 peaks are often driven by high FiO2, sedation, or sampling variability and are not a typical trigger for AKI. Extremes on the high end are less informative for renal hypoperfusion than low minima, especially early post-op.
A high SvO2 maximum is generally nonspecific and may reflect reduced extraction or high oxygen delivery rather than renal risk. It is less clinically linked to AKI than low SvO2 episodes.

Low SvO2 minima indicate episodes of inadequate oxygen delivery/low cardiac output or shock, which can directly reduce renal perfusion and precipitate AKI. This is a relevant acute-insult marker, though early post-op fluctuations are common (0–12h cap).

Low SvO2 minima during 13–24h suggest persistent or recurrent low cardiac output/shock physiology, strongly linked to renal hypoperfusion and subsequent AKI. The minimum captures the pathologic insult better than mean/max in this epoch.

More frequent SvO2 checks can reflect clinician concern for hemodynamic instability, which may correlate with AKI risk, but it is not a physiologic mechanism. As a behavioral proxy it should remain low.

Increased SvO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h may indicate ongoing concern for low cardiac output or shock, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still, it is a care-intensity proxy rather than a direct biomarker.

Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern/monitoring for low cardiac output or shock, which can precede AKI, but it is not a physiologic value itself. As a _measured feature it should remain low-importance.

Maintenance IV volume relates indirectly to intravascular volume and renal perfusion, but early post-op fluids are routine and mean values smooth clinically important boluses. Collinearity rule: mean is less informative than extremes, so capped.

Ongoing maintenance fluids may reflect evolving hemodynamics, but mean volume is a weak proxy for true volume status and is less informative than extremes. Capped to avoid redundancy with max/min aggregations.

Closer to outcome, sustained maintenance fluid needs can correlate with instability, but mean maintenance volume still poorly captures fluid responsiveness/overload compared with extremes. Capped per collinearity rule.

Higher peak maintenance IV rates can indicate hypotension/vasoplegia management and attempts to support renal perfusion, which relates to AKI risk. In 0–12h many peaks are routine post-bypass, so conservatively scored (max 6).

A high peak maintenance IV rate in the trajectory phase may signal persistent vasoplegia/low effective circulating volume, both associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Still indirect because it reflects treatment rather than measured perfusion.

Late high peak maintenance IV requirements suggest ongoing hemodynamic instability or third-spacing with attempts to maintain perfusion, which strongly associates with subsequent creatinine rise. This window is closest to the 60h creatinine outcome.

Low minimum maintenance IV rate early post-op is often protocol-driven (e.g., keep-vein-open) and not specific for renal perfusion or AKI. The minimum is less likely to capture a harmful insult than the maximum.
A low minimum maintenance rate is usually a floor set by nursing/ICU protocols and does not reliably indicate euvolemia or renal risk. Weak and nonspecific association with AKI.

Later low minimum maintenance fluids could reflect fluid restriction in overload/heart failure, which can coexist with AKI risk, but it remains an indirect and noisy proxy. Minimum values are generally less informative than peaks.
Measurement frequency is primarily documentation/monitoring behavior and not a physiologic driver of AKI. Early post-op fluid charting is routine for nearly all patients.
How often maintenance IV volume is recorded reflects workflow rather than renal pathophysiology. As a _measured feature it provides minimal specific prognostic value for AKI.
More frequent fluid documentation later may reflect higher acuity or active titration, which can correlate with AKI risk, but it remains a behavioral proxy. Kept low per _measured rule.

Having PAP monitoring suggests higher-risk cardiac dysfunction/right heart strain, which can contribute to renal congestion and AKI, but this is only a monitoring indicator. Early post-op invasive monitoring is common, so conservative.

Continued PAP monitoring into 13–24h may indicate persistent hemodynamic concern (RV failure, pulmonary HTN) that can predispose to AKI via low output/venous congestion. Still indirect because it is presence of measurement, not the PAP value.

PAP monitoring persisting to 25–36h often marks ongoing cardiopulmonary instability (e.g., RV failure) linked to renal hypoperfusion and congestion, increasing AKI risk. Nonetheless it remains a proxy for severity rather than a direct biomarker.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op (0–12h) invasive monitoring is common and not specific for impending AKI.
Ordering/recording frequency is a behavioral proxy for illness severity, not a direct driver of AKI. In 13–24h it may reflect concern for hemodynamic instability but remains nonspecific.
More frequent PAP monitoring late (25–36h) can indicate persistent shock/right heart failure management, which can precede AKI, but it is still an indirect proxy rather than physiology.
CVP measurement frequency mainly reflects use of central access and monitoring intensity. In the immediate post-op window it is routine and poorly specific for AKI risk.

More frequent CVP documentation may correlate with ongoing instability or fluid management complexity, but it is not a direct renal insult marker. As a measured feature it should remain low importance.
Late frequent CVP monitoring can signal persistent venous congestion/need for vasoactive titration, which is associated with AKI risk, but frequency itself is only an indirect indicator.

Mean EtCO2 is a nonspecific marker influenced by ventilation settings and perfusion; sustained low values can reflect low cardiac output. In 0–12h it is often transient and less predictive of later AKI.

Persistently abnormal mean EtCO2 may reflect ongoing low pulmonary blood flow/low cardiac output, which can reduce renal perfusion. However, mean values smooth short hypotensive insults and are less specific than extremes.
In the 25–36h window, sustained low EtCO2 can indicate ongoing low cardiac output or poor perfusion, both linked to AKI. Still indirect and confounded by ventilator management.

High EtCO2 is more often related to hypoventilation or increased CO2 production rather than renal hypoperfusion. In 0–12h it is commonly driven by ventilator/anesthesia effects and is not a strong AKI signal.
EtCO2 maxima generally reflect ventilatory adequacy rather than kidney injury risk. It is not a recognized trigger/marker for AKI compared with low-perfusion indicators.
Even close to outcome, elevated EtCO2 is not a direct biomarker of renal dysfunction and is typically ventilator-related. It provides limited incremental AKI prediction versus low-perfusion measures.

Very low EtCO2 can reflect acute low cardiac output, pulmonary embolic physiology, or severe hypotension—states that can precipitate renal ischemia. Capped at 6 due to the 0–12h epoch where transient derangements are common.

A low EtCO2 nadir in 13–24h suggests episodic hypoperfusion/low cardiac output beyond the immediate post-op period, which is strongly linked to AKI risk. Extremes capture acute insults better than means.

A low EtCO2 nadir close to hour 60 is a strong leading indicator of ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion/shock, a key pathway to ischemic AKI. As an extreme, it better captures clinically meaningful insults than the mean.

Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity and ventilator management rather than renal physiology. In the immediate post-op period EtCO2 is routinely monitored, offering minimal AKI-specific signal.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. More frequent ETCO2 checks may correlate with instability but is nonspecific for AKI.
Represents clinician attention/ventilation monitoring rather than kidney perfusion directly. Could weakly proxy ongoing critical illness but is not a direct AKI predictor.

Higher average FiO2 suggests respiratory failure/poor oxygen delivery which can contribute to renal ischemia, but early post-op oxygen needs are common and transient. Mean smooths extremes and is less tied to acute insult.

High peak FiO2 in the immediate post-op period can indicate severe hypoxemia/shock physiology that threatens renal oxygen delivery. As an extreme it better captures acute insult than the mean, but early epoch caps overall importance.
Low minimum FiO2 generally reflects stability and is not a trigger for AKI. It is less informative than high FiO2 requirements for identifying physiologic stress.
Frequency of FiO2 documentation/orders is a behavioral proxy for acuity and ventilator titration, not a renal mechanism. Early post-op frequent measurements are routine.
Primarily reflects ongoing ventilator management intensity rather than kidney injury biology. May weakly correlate with severity of illness but is nonspecific.
Measurement frequency is not a physiologic driver of AKI; it mainly indicates clinician concern/instability. Even close to outcome, it remains an indirect proxy.

Higher average tidal volumes can contribute to ventilator-induced lung injury and inflammatory/venous return effects that may worsen renal perfusion, but this is indirect. Mean values are less informative than extremes and early post-op settings are often protocolized.

Sustained higher tidal volumes may reflect ongoing mechanical ventilation and systemic inflammation, indirectly increasing AKI risk. However, mean tidal volume is still a weak, non-specific predictor compared with hemodynamics and renal labs.

Persistently higher tidal volumes closer to hour 60 may indicate ongoing respiratory failure and higher intrathoracic pressures affecting renal venous congestion/perfusion. Still indirect and less pathognomonic than oxygenation/hemodynamic extremes.

Peak tidal volumes can capture episodes of aggressive ventilation that may impair venous return and increase inflammatory stress, plausibly contributing to AKI. Early post-op variability and protocol-driven ventilation limit specificity.

High tidal volume spikes during the trajectory phase may reflect worsening lung mechanics or ventilator dyssynchrony with potential hemodynamic consequences. Still an indirect and relatively weak AKI predictor.

Late high tidal volume extremes may indicate persistent severe respiratory failure or high airway pressures with adverse cardiopulmonary-renal interactions (reduced cardiac output/renal perfusion, venous congestion). As an extreme, it is more informative than the mean for capturing insult.
Low minimum tidal volume is usually benign (lung-protective ventilation) and not a known AKI trigger. It is less informative than high tidal volume extremes for physiologic stress.
Lower minimum tidal volumes generally reflect lung-protective strategy or weaning and do not directly predict AKI. It provides little signal compared with markers of hypoperfusion or nephrotoxins.

Low tidal volumes may reflect lung-protective ventilation or reduced respiratory reserve; any AKI link is indirect via severity of illness and hemodynamics. Not a primary renal perfusion driver compared with MAP/vasopressors.
Measurement frequency is mainly a proxy for monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Early post-op ventilation monitoring is routine after cardiac surgery.
Represents clinician monitoring behavior more than physiology; may weakly correlate with instability but is nonspecific for AKI. Ventilator checks remain common in this phase.
More frequent ventilator documentation late may indicate ongoing critical illness or failure to wean, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, not a direct renal predictor.
ABP pulse measurement frequency reflects invasive monitoring and acuity, not kidney injury itself. In the immediate post-op window this is common standard-of-care.
Primarily indicates continued arterial line monitoring; weakly associated with instability but nonspecific for renal outcomes. As a _measured feature it should remain low importance.
Persistent frequent ABP monitoring at 25–36h may signal ongoing hemodynamic concern, which can precede AKI. Still indirect and behavioral, so capped low.
Heart rate measurement frequency is routine in ICU and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or injury. As a behavioral proxy in the immediate post-op period it adds little AKI-specific signal.
Ongoing HR monitoring is standard and not specific for AKI risk. Measurement frequency provides minimal incremental information beyond other severity markers.
Late increased HR documentation could reflect instability (arrhythmias, shock) that may relate to AKI risk, but it remains nonspecific. As a _measured feature it should stay low.
Frequency of MAP measurement reflects monitoring intensity; early post-cardiac surgery this is routine with arterial lines. It is not a direct physiologic measure of hypotension burden.
Continued MAP monitoring suggests acuity but is not itself a renal insult marker. As a _measured feature it should remain low importance.
More frequent MAP checks closer to outcome may indicate hemodynamic instability, which is relevant to AKI risk, but this is still indirect. Behavioral proxy only, so limited importance.
Respiration measurement frequency during/after anesthesia is routine and not mechanistically linked to AKI. As a _measured feature in the immediate period it is minimally informative.
Ongoing respiratory rate documentation is standard ICU care and not AKI-specific. Measurement frequency adds little beyond overall illness severity.
Late frequent respiratory rate documentation may reflect ongoing ventilatory support or distress, indirectly correlating with severity and AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so low importance.

Measurement frequency reflects routine monitoring rather than a renal mechanism, and is nearly universal post-cardiac surgery. In the immediate 0–12h period it mainly captures standard care/acuity, not AKI physiology.
Ordering/recording frequency is a behavioral proxy and usually routine in ICU patients. It has no direct link to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity.

More frequent pulse oximetry documentation may reflect ongoing respiratory instability and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still, it is not a physiologic renal marker and remains an indirect proxy.

Tube intake measurement frequency is largely care-process related and may reflect feeding initiation/monitoring rather than kidney injury. Any association with AKI would be indirect via overall acuity.
More frequent tube intake tracking may indicate closer monitoring in sicker patients, but it is not a driver of renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Thus it is a weak, indirect proxy.
At 25–36h, continued intensive intake tracking can reflect ongoing instability, but it still does not measure renal function or perfusion. Expected predictive value for creatinine rise is low.

Average maintenance bolus volume is an imprecise surrogate for hemodynamic instability and resuscitation needs, which can relate to AKI risk. However, mean is collinear with extremes and less reflective of acute hypotensive/resuscitation episodes.

A high maximum bolus volume in 0–12h suggests acute resuscitation for hypotension/low cardiac output, a known trigger for renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. Capped at 6 due to the immediate post-op window where transient derangements are common.
Minimum bolus volume is not clinically meaningful for AKI risk and does not capture hypoperfusion or shock. It is largely redundant/noise compared with max volume.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior/documentation intensity rather than a renal mechanism. It may weakly proxy acuity but is not a direct predictor of creatinine rise.
More frequent bolus documentation can indicate ongoing instability, but it remains a process measure rather than physiology. Therefore it has weak, indirect relevance to AKI.

In the 25–36h leading window, frequent bolus recording may reflect persistent hemodynamic problems prompting resuscitation, which correlates with AKI risk. Still, it is a behavioral proxy and should remain low.

Need for pacing can indicate conduction disease or post-op low cardiac output states that may reduce renal perfusion. Mean pacing over 0–12h is less informative than presence/need and is limited by the early transient post-op period.

Persistent pacing requirement into 13–24h suggests ongoing cardiac dysfunction or conduction issues that can impair renal perfusion and raise AKI risk. Mean pacing is still an indirect marker rather than a renal biomarker.

Ongoing pacing requirement at 25–36h can be a marker of sustained cardiac dysfunction/low output physiology, which is a recognized contributor to post-cardiac surgery AKI. It is a stronger leading indicator due to temporal proximity to the 60h creatinine outcome.

Any pacing requirement (max) in the immediate post-op period can indicate significant conduction disturbance or hemodynamic compromise, both linked to renal hypoperfusion. Score capped at 6 due to the 0–12h epoch where transient pacing is common.

Need for pacing suggests conduction disease/low-output physiology that can reduce renal perfusion, but it is an indirect marker and often transient post-op. Max is preferred over mean, but overall linkage to AKI is modest in this epoch.
Persistent need for pacing closer to hour 60 can indicate ongoing low cardiac output or hemodynamic instability, which increases AKI risk. Still an indirect proxy rather than a renal biomarker.
Early post-op pacing is common and may reflect transient conduction issues rather than sustained hypoperfusion. In the 0–12h epoch, interpret conservatively and as an indirect AKI signal.
Ongoing pacing requirement into 13–24h may reflect failure to stabilize hemodynamics, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However, it remains a nonspecific surrogate.

Continued pacing in the 25–36h window suggests persistent cardiac dysfunction/instability, a recognized contributor to postoperative AKI. It is still indirect and may correlate with other hemodynamic variables.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a direct physiologic driver of AKI. Early post-op monitoring is routine, limiting specificity.
More frequent documentation/assessment of pacing is a behavioral proxy for instability but not a mechanism of kidney injury. It may add limited incremental signal beyond whether pacing is present.

Increased pacing-related measurement closer to the outcome can reflect clinician concern for ongoing instability, which correlates with AKI risk. Still primarily a surveillance/acuity marker rather than causal.

Any CRRT/SLED-related maintenance implies severe illness and possible early renal dysfunction, but the '_measured' field is a process-of-care proxy. In 0–12h, initiation/adjustment may be perioperative and not yet predictive of hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Ongoing CRRT/SLED maintenance monitoring suggests significant renal/hemodynamic derangement, but measurement frequency is not a direct biomarker. It may reflect clinician response rather than kidney injury severity.

Closer to hour 60, frequent CRRT/SLED maintenance measurements can indicate persistent renal failure/instability, but it remains a behavioral proxy. The presence of RRT itself would be more informative than how often pump rate is recorded.
Sevoflurane exposure/recording is largely routine anesthetic care and not a strong independent predictor of postoperative AKI in this context. Measurement frequency mainly reflects documentation.

Sevoflurane is typically no longer a key ICU exposure post-op; measurement frequency is unlikely to track renal risk. Any association would be confounded by procedural factors rather than kidney physiology.
Sevoflurane measurement at 25–36h is uncommon and would mostly reflect documentation anomalies or unusual sedation practices, not a direct AKI mechanism. Thus it has minimal prognostic value.
Frequency of documenting IV intake reflects nursing/ICU workflow and acuity rather than renal physiology. In the immediate post-op period, high measurement frequency is routine and nonspecific.
More frequent IV intake measurements may correlate with instability or fluid management complexity, but it is not a direct driver of AKI. As a process measure, it should remain low-importance.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician attention/acuity rather than a direct renal mechanism. Higher monitoring may correlate with instability, but it is an indirect proxy.
Drain output measurement frequency is largely routine post-op care and not a direct driver/marker of renal injury. In the immediate 0–12h window it is especially nonspecific.
Ongoing drain output checks are standard ICU/post-cardiac surgery practice and do not specifically indicate AKI risk. Frequency reflects workflow more than physiology.
Later continued drain monitoring may weakly proxy ongoing bleeding/complications that could affect perfusion, but it remains a behavioral/management signal rather than a renal biomarker.
This appears to be an administrative/timing documentation variable with no physiologic link to kidney injury. Not informative for AKI prediction.
Administrative/timing documentation does not reflect renal perfusion, nephrotoxins, or kidney function. No meaningful AKI signal expected.
Administrative/timing documentation variable; lacks a plausible causal or biomarker relationship to creatinine rise. Should not contribute to AKI prediction.

Loss of sinus rhythm can correlate with hemodynamic instability and renal hypoperfusion, but in the immediate post-op period arrhythmias are common/transient. Mean proportion in 0–12h is a weak indirect signal.
Persistent non-sinus rhythm into 13–24h may indicate ongoing instability and reduced renal perfusion risk. However, the mean is collinear with extremes and less sensitive to acute insults.

By 25–36h, sustained rhythm status may reflect recovery vs ongoing instability, which can affect renal perfusion. Still, the mean is less informative than an extreme/any-episode indicator for acute events.
A max indicator for being in sinus at any point is not a strong AKI predictor (many patients will have at least some sinus rhythm). In 0–12h it is particularly nonspecific.
Having any sinus rhythm during the window is common and does not capture the burden of arrhythmia-related hypoperfusion. Provides limited incremental AKI signal.
Any-episode sinus rhythm is not a strong discriminator of hemodynamic compromise by 25–36h. More informative would be absence of sinus or sustained arrhythmia burden rather than max.

A min indicator (i.e., any time not in sinus rhythm) better captures occurrence of arrhythmia, which can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. Capped by 0–12h epoch where transient arrhythmias are common (max 6).

Any non-sinus rhythm persisting into 13–24h suggests ongoing instability and potential low-flow states that increase AKI risk. The min aggregation captures the clinically relevant extreme better than mean/max.

Any non-sinus rhythm in the 25–36h leading window can indicate persistent hemodynamic compromise (e.g., AF with RVR, junctional rhythm) that threatens renal perfusion. Min is the most pathology-relevant aggregation among the rhythm summaries.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology; normal sinus rhythm documentation is routine post-op. Weak proxy for acuity only.
Primarily a behavioral/monitoring artifact rather than a mechanistic AKI driver. Limited incremental signal for creatinine rise.
Counts of rhythm assessments are not a direct predictor of renal injury and mostly reflect routine ICU surveillance. At most a weak proxy for instability.

Sinus rhythm variants are generally benign and not a primary cause of renal hypoperfusion; early post-op rhythm variability is common/transient. Mean aggregation further dilutes any brief hemodynamic impact.
Sustained sinus-variant rhythm is rarely a meaningful determinant of renal perfusion compared with hypotension/low output states. Mean value is unlikely to capture clinically important insults.

Persistent rhythm irregularity could indirectly reflect autonomic stress or evolving cardiac dysfunction, but sinus variants are typically low-risk. Mean aggregation makes it a weak, indirect predictor.

Any occurrence of sinus-variant rhythm in the immediate post-op window is common and usually hemodynamically tolerated. As an extreme/any-event flag it may weakly track instability but is not a direct AKI trigger (cap due to limited pathology specificity).

An episode of sinus-variant rhythm may correlate with overall physiologic stress but is not a strong driver of renal ischemia. Better predictors would be hypotension/vasopressor need; thus limited importance.

Late (25–36h) occurrence could be a mild marker of ongoing cardiac irritability/instability closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect and typically not hemodynamically significant, so kept modest.
A minimum/absence indicator for a benign rhythm variant has no clear mechanistic link to AKI. Provides little clinical signal beyond redundancy with other rhythm features.
Minimum/absence of sinus-variant rhythm is not informative for renal perfusion or tubular injury. Likely redundant with max/mean rhythm indicators.
Absence of a generally benign rhythm variant does not predict creatinine rise. Minimal physiologic relevance and high redundancy.
Measurement frequency is a proxy for monitoring/annotation rather than kidney injury. Early post-op rhythm checks are routine and not AKI-specific.
Counts of rhythm documentation reflect clinician behavior and telemetry practices, not renal pathophysiology. Weak proxy for acuity at best.
More frequent rhythm documentation late may indicate perceived instability, which can correlate with low cardiac output and AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, so kept low per rules.

Atrial arrhythmias (e.g., AF) can reduce cardiac output and prompt vasopressors/diuresis, indirectly increasing AKI risk, but are common immediately post-op and often transient. Mean aggregation and early epoch limit specificity (max 6 in 0–12h).

Atrial arrhythmias can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion, but the mean burden is less informative than whether it occurred at all in this window. Capped to limit redundancy with max/min indicators.

Later atrial arrhythmia burden may reflect ongoing hemodynamic instability that can contribute to AKI, but mean burden is still less pathophysiologically specific than presence/episodes. Capped for collinearity with max/min.

Any atrial arrhythmia early after surgery can transiently lower forward flow and renal perfusion, increasing AKI risk. In the immediate 0–12h period this is common and often transient, so conservatively scored.

Atrial arrhythmia persisting/occurring in the trajectory phase suggests failure to stabilize and can impair renal perfusion via reduced cardiac output and need for vasoactive support. More prognostic than in the immediate post-op window.

Atrial arrhythmia close to the 60h creatinine outcome is a strong marker of ongoing hemodynamic stress and potential low-output state, both key AKI drivers after cardiac surgery. Presence in this leading window is clinically meaningful.
The minimum (often 0) mainly reflects time without arrhythmia and is not a physiologic insult marker. It adds little beyond the max indicator and is likely redundant.
Minimum arrhythmia value is not an AKI trigger and largely encodes absence of arrhythmia at some point. Redundant with max/presence features.
Minimum value does not capture the hemodynamic insult; it mostly indicates there was at least some time without arrhythmia. Redundant with max/presence.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring/clinical attention rather than a direct renal mechanism. Early post-op rhythm monitoring is routine, so it is a weak proxy for acuity.
How often rhythm status is recorded is primarily a behavioral proxy and may reflect nursing/ICU workflow more than AKI biology. Limited incremental value for predicting creatinine rise.

Increased measurement frequency late may indicate clinician concern for instability/arrhythmia recurrence, which can correlate with higher illness severity and AKI risk. Still indirect and scored low per rule.

Need for pacing can indicate conduction disease or post-op low-output physiology, which may relate to renal hypoperfusion, but mean pacing burden is less specific than presence. Conservative in the immediate post-op window.
Ongoing pacing requirement may reflect persistent cardiac dysfunction and hemodynamic vulnerability, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Mean burden is capped to reduce redundancy with max/presence.

Late pacing dependence can mark continued cardiac dysfunction, but mean pacing burden is not a direct renal biomarker and is less informative than whether pacing is required at all. Capped for collinearity.

Any pacing in the first 12h can signal perioperative conduction issues or low-output state that may compromise renal perfusion. However, early pacing is common and often transient post-cardiac surgery.

Pacing requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent conduction/hemodynamic problems rather than transient immediate post-op effects, which can be associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk. More prognostic than 0–12h.
Need for pacing can reflect conduction disease/low cardiac output risk, which can secondarily reduce renal perfusion. It is an indirect marker and not a direct AKI driver.
Immediate post-op pacing is common/transient after cardiac surgery and often resolves without sustained hemodynamic impact. Indirect link to AKI via low output only.
Persistent pacing requirement into 13–24h may indicate ongoing conduction issues or instability that can impair renal perfusion. Still an indirect proxy rather than a renal-specific predictor.

Ongoing pacing need close to the outcome window suggests persistent conduction/low-output physiology and higher risk of renal hypoperfusion. Moderate established association but not definitive for AKI.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Early post-op rhythm monitoring is routine and only weakly signals AKI risk.
More frequent documentation/assessment is mainly a proxy for clinician concern/acuity. It is not a direct mechanism for kidney injury.
Increased monitoring late may reflect ongoing instability prompting closer observation, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavioral proxy, not a physiologic trigger.
Average burden of heart block in the first 12h is often transient post-op and may not translate to sustained renal hypoperfusion. Mean aggregation also dilutes brief high-risk episodes.
Sustained heart block burden can contribute to bradycardia/low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Mean remains less informative than presence of any episode (max).

Ongoing heart block closer to hour 60 can indicate persistent hemodynamic vulnerability affecting kidney perfusion. Mean aggregation is capped due to collinearity with max capturing acute episodes better.
Any episode of heart block early post-op can cause transient hypotension/bradycardia and renal hypoperfusion, but early derangements are common and often corrected. Capped by 0–12h epoch limit.

Heart block occurring in 13–24h suggests persistent conduction problems and higher likelihood of low-output states that predispose to AKI. Presence/absence (max) better captures clinically meaningful events than mean.

Heart block episodes in 25–36h are a strong marker of ongoing instability and potential low cardiac output, which directly threatens renal perfusion. This late-window extreme is more predictive than averaged burden.

A minimum indicator for heart block (often effectively 'none') provides little additional information beyond max and is prone to redundancy. Early post-op period also has many transient rhythm issues.
Minimum aggregation is not clinically informative for AKI risk compared with whether any heart block occurred (max). It adds collinearity without capturing pathology.
Minimum heart block status is largely redundant and does not represent an insult or severity. Max better captures clinically relevant episodes linked to renal hypoperfusion.

Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring/clinical attention rather than a direct renal mechanism. Early post-op conduction issues can correlate with instability, but this is an indirect proxy.
Counts of documented/checked heart block reflect clinician concern and monitoring intensity more than kidney perfusion. Persistent conduction problems may track with low output, but still indirect.
Later frequent assessment of heart block may indicate ongoing hemodynamic compromise (e.g., bradycardia/low cardiac output) that can reduce renal perfusion. Still a behavioral proxy, so capped low.

Myocardial ischemia can lead to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, but mean over the immediate post-op window is diluted and often transient. Collinearity with max/min makes mean less informative.

Sustained ischemic changes may signal evolving cardiac dysfunction affecting renal perfusion, but the mean aggregation blunts episodic ischemia. Prefer extremes for capturing clinically meaningful insults.
Ongoing ischemia closer to outcome could matter for renal perfusion, but mean remains a smoothed metric and is redundant with max/min. Therefore capped to limit collinearity.

Any peak ischemic ST change suggests perioperative myocardial ischemia that can precipitate low output and renal hypoperfusion. In the 0–12h window this may be transient post-bypass, so conservatively scored.
Peak ischemic episodes during the trajectory phase more strongly suggest ongoing myocardial injury/low output physiology that can drive AKI. Max captures the acute insult better than mean.

Late peak ischemic ST changes are a strong marker of ongoing myocardial ischemia and potential cardiogenic shock/renal hypoperfusion near the outcome window. Extremes are clinically meaningful triggers for AKI risk.
A minimum (i.e., most negative) ST deviation can indicate ischemia, but interpretation depends on lead/direction and may be noisy post-op. Early window changes are often transient, so kept moderate.

More extreme ST deviation during 13–24h can reflect clinically significant ischemia with downstream low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Min captures an extreme better than mean but is less straightforward than max.

Late extreme ST deviation suggests ongoing ischemia close to the creatinine outcome and can be associated with hemodynamic compromise affecting kidneys. As an extreme metric it is more informative than mean.
Frequency of documenting/assessing ST changes is primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity rather than renal pathophysiology. Early post-op ECG surveillance is common and not specific for AKI.
More frequent ST assessments may indicate clinician concern for ischemia/instability, but it remains a behavioral signal with weak specificity for AKI. Thus kept low.
In the later window, increased ST monitoring may reflect ongoing ischemia or instability that can threaten renal perfusion. Still not a physiologic measurement, so limited to a low score.

Non-specific rhythm abnormalities can correlate with perioperative stress and hemodynamic instability, which may indirectly affect renal perfusion. As a mean and a heterogeneous category, it is weakly informative and potentially noisy.

Non-sinus/atypical rhythms can correlate with hemodynamic instability and low cardiac output, indirectly increasing AKI risk. As a mean over the window it is less specific than an extreme/occurrence indicator and may be collinear with max/min rhythm flags.

Persistent atypical rhythm closer to hour 60 may reflect ongoing instability or need for interventions that can reduce renal perfusion. Mean aggregation is still a blunt proxy compared with an occurrence/extreme indicator.

Any occurrence of an atypical rhythm immediately post-op can signal acute instability and potential low cardiac output affecting renal perfusion. Early post-bypass arrhythmias are common/transient, so capped by the 0–12h max score rule.
Occurrence of atypical rhythm in the trajectory phase suggests failure to stabilize and possible ongoing hemodynamic compromise, increasing AKI risk. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker.

Atypical rhythm occurring in the 25–36h window is a stronger leading indicator of persistent low output/instability and vasopressor need, which are closely linked to AKI after cardiac surgery. Captures an acute insult better than mean.

Presence of atypical rhythm at any point in the early window is only modestly informative because transient post-op rhythm disturbances are frequent. Less clinically interpretable than a max/occurrence flag for acute events.
Suggests atypical rhythm is present across the window, but the min definition is less intuitive and may be redundant with max. Indicates possible sustained instability with indirect AKI risk.

If the rhythm is persistently atypical across the 25–36h window it may reflect sustained hemodynamic compromise, which can reduce renal perfusion. Still an indirect marker compared with direct hemodynamic or renal labs.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a physiologic mechanism of AKI. Early post-op rhythm monitoring is routine, limiting specificity.
More frequent rhythm documentation may indicate clinical concern or instability, but it is a behavioral proxy and not a direct AKI driver. Kept low per _measured rule.
Increased rhythm measurement late may reflect ongoing instability prompting closer surveillance, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable, so limited to low importance.

Ventilator mode choice (ASV) is not a direct renal risk factor; it mainly reflects respiratory management strategy. In the immediate post-op period it is common and weakly related to AKI except via overall illness severity.

Continued need for controlled/assisted ventilation mode may indicate greater severity of illness and hemodynamic/respiratory compromise that can contribute to AKI. Mean mode exposure remains an indirect proxy.

Any use of ASV early post-op may reflect delayed extubation or respiratory support needs, indirectly associated with higher AKI risk via severity and hemodynamics. Early window limits strength of inference.
ASV use during 13–24h may indicate ongoing ventilatory dependence and higher acuity, which correlates with AKI risk after cardiac surgery. Still indirect and not a renal-specific marker.
Min aggregation for a ventilator mode is hard to interpret clinically and likely redundant with max/mean indicators. Vent mode itself has only an indirect relationship to AKI.
ASV use is more a marker of ventilatory strategy than a direct renal insult. In the 13–24h window it may weakly reflect ongoing respiratory/cardiac instability but is not a primary AKI driver.
Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring behavior rather than physiology. Early post-op ordering/recording of vent mode is routine and not specific for AKI risk.
More frequent recording may indicate higher acuity or ventilator adjustments, which can correlate with illness severity. Still an indirect behavioral proxy and not mechanistically linked to AKI.

Increased measurement frequency late may reflect persistent instability and clinician concern, indirectly associated with AKI risk. As a _measured feature it remains a weak proxy rather than a causal factor.

Controlled ventilation early post-op is common and often transient; it mainly reflects immediate post-anesthesia/CPB course. Mean use over 0–12h is a coarse severity marker and not directly kidney-specific.

Persistent need for controlled ventilation in 13–24h suggests slower recovery, possible hemodynamic/respiratory failure and higher illness severity, which increases AKI risk. As a mean aggregation it is less specific than an extreme indicator.

Any occurrence of controlled ventilation in the immediate 0–12h period can indicate higher early support needs, which may correlate with perioperative shock/CPB effects. However this window is noisy and transient, so capped conservatively.

Any controlled ventilation during 13–24h more strongly suggests ongoing critical illness and potential hemodynamic compromise, which is a known contributor to AKI. Binary max captures the presence of this higher-support state better than the mean.

A minimum indicator for a binary vent-mode variable is not clinically informative (often reflects whether it was ever absent). It does not capture a renal-relevant insult and is prone to redundancy with max/mean.
As with 0–12h, the minimum of a binary vent-mode variable is poorly interpretable and largely redundant. It provides little specific information about AKI risk beyond other aggregations.
Frequency of recording controlled mode is a behavioral/documentation proxy and is routine in the immediate post-op period. It is not a direct physiologic predictor of creatinine rise.
More frequent documentation may reflect ventilator instability and higher acuity, indirectly associated with AKI. Still a weak proxy and constrained by the _measured rule.
Late frequent recording of controlled mode may indicate persistent critical illness and clinician concern, which correlates with AKI risk. As a _measured feature it remains indirect and capped low.
SIMV use early post-op is common during weaning and does not directly affect renal perfusion. Mean use in 0–12h is a weak severity/weaning marker and not kidney-specific.

SIMV in 13–24h may reflect ongoing ventilatory dependence or slower weaning, indirectly tracking illness severity that can predispose to AKI. As a mean aggregation it is less specific than an extreme indicator.

Any SIMV use in 0–12h suggests the patient required ventilatory support/weaning mode early, which weakly correlates with postoperative severity. This period is highly confounded by routine post-op ventilation, so kept modest.

Need for SIMV suggests ongoing ventilatory support and illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk via hemodynamic/oxygenation stress. As a ventilator-mode indicator (not a renal/perfusion variable) it is an indirect proxy and capped low to avoid redundancy with other vent-mode summaries.

Early post-op SIMV use is common and heavily influenced by anesthesia/CPB recovery, making it a weak and nonspecific AKI predictor in the 0–12h window (conservative scoring). It mainly reflects routine immediate post-op ventilation rather than renal physiology.

Persistent SIMV requirement into 13–24h may indicate delayed recovery or cardiopulmonary instability, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still a nonspecific severity marker rather than a direct renal insult.
Measurement frequency reflects documentation/monitoring behavior rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is routine ICU care and provides minimal specific signal for impending AKI.
More frequent recording may reflect higher acuity or ventilator adjustments, but it remains a behavioral proxy rather than a mechanism for AKI. Limited specificity for renal outcomes.
Increased measurement frequency late may indicate ongoing instability, but it is still a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct driver of creatinine rise. Kept low per _measured rule.

Spontaneous mode early can indicate faster recovery and potentially less hemodynamic stress, indirectly lowering AKI risk. However, in 0–12h this is often transient and protocol-driven, so predictive value is limited.
Sustained spontaneous ventilation by 13–24h suggests improving trajectory and lower severity, which can correlate with lower AKI risk. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker.

Any ability to tolerate spontaneous mode in the immediate post-op period may reflect better cardiopulmonary reserve and less shock exposure, indirectly protective for kidneys. Conservative cap due to early epoch and indirectness.

Achieving spontaneous mode at any point in 13–24h can indicate stabilization and reduced need for sedatives/vasopressors, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. Still a proxy for overall recovery rather than kidney-specific physiology.
Minimum spontaneous mode in 0–12h largely reflects immediate post-op management and sedation practices, with weak specificity for AKI. It is not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or injury.

If the patient never reaches spontaneous mode during 13–24h, it may indicate persistent critical illness and higher AKI risk via ongoing hemodynamic/oxygenation stress. Still indirect and less informative than direct perfusion/lab variables.
Frequency of documenting spontaneous mode is a behavioral/monitoring artifact and routine in early post-op care. It does not represent a physiological pathway to AKI.
More frequent documentation may reflect weaning attempts or instability, but remains a nonspecific behavioral proxy. Limited direct relevance to creatinine rise.
Late measurement frequency may track ongoing ventilator management intensity, but it is not a mechanistic AKI predictor. Scored low per _measured rule.

Mean arterial pressure relates to renal perfusion; sustained hypotension increases AKI risk, but in 0–12h MAP is often transiently affected by anesthesia/CPB and aggressive titration. Also, mean is less sensitive than hypotensive minima for capturing renal ischemic insults, so importance is capped.
Sustained MAP relates to renal perfusion, but the mean smooths over hypotensive episodes that more directly drive ischemic AKI; prefer MAP_min for pathology.
Later sustained perfusion pressure is relevant to ongoing renal blood flow, but mean MAP can miss brief but harmful hypotension; MAP_min is typically more predictive.
Hypertensive peaks are not a typical direct trigger for postoperative AKI compared with hypotension/low flow; early post-op variability is common.
High MAP episodes are generally less informative for AKI risk than low MAP; may reflect vasopressor titration rather than renal insult.
Late hypertension is not a primary mechanism for AKI in this setting; low MAP/renal hypoperfusion is the key concern.
Early postoperative hypotension/low perfusion is a known contributor to ischemic tubular injury, but immediate post-CPB instability is common and transient (cap at 6 for 0–12h).
Persistent or recurrent hypotension in the trajectory phase strongly predicts renal hypoperfusion and subsequent creatinine rise.
Low MAP close to the outcome window is a strong leading indicator of ongoing renal hypoperfusion/shock physiology and is highly predictive of AKI-related creatinine increase.
Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic mechanism; arterial lines are routine after cardiac surgery.
Arterial-line MAP measurement count is largely standard-of-care and mainly a proxy for documentation/acuity, not renal injury.
Continued frequent MAP recording is expected in ICU care and does not directly indicate AKI risk beyond the MAP values themselves.
NPO status is routine immediately post-cardiac surgery and has no direct physiologic link to AKI beyond general illness severity.
Prolonged NPO may weakly proxy delayed recovery/greater severity, but it is not a direct driver of renal dysfunction.
Still being NPO later may indicate ongoing critical illness or ventilation, indirectly associated with AKI risk but nonspecific.
Being NPO at any point in the first 12 hours is expected post-op and provides minimal discriminatory information for AKI.
Any NPO during 13–24h may reflect slower stabilization and indirectly higher AKI risk, but it is a weak, nonspecific proxy.

Still being NPO at 25–36h can be a proxy for ongoing critical illness, intubation, or hemodynamic instability, which correlates with AKI risk. It is not a direct renal mechanism and is largely confounded by severity of illness.

Being NPO in the immediate 0–12h post-cardiac surgery period is routine and provides little specific information about renal injury risk. This is standard postoperative care rather than a kidney-related signal.
Remaining NPO during 13–24h may reflect delayed recovery or ongoing support needs, indirectly associated with AKI. However, it is nonspecific and heavily confounded by overall acuity.

NPO status persisting into 25–36h can indicate continued critical illness or inability to extubate, which often co-travels with renal hypoperfusion risk. It remains an indirect proxy rather than a causal renal predictor.
Measurement frequency of NPO status mainly reflects documentation/ordering behavior and routine ICU workflow. It does not represent a physiologic driver of AKI.
How often NPO status is recorded is a behavioral/administrative signal and is not mechanistically linked to kidney injury. At most it weakly reflects monitoring intensity.

More frequent documentation of NPO status could weakly proxy higher acuity or ongoing procedures, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still, it is not a physiologic marker and should be low importance.
Diet status in the first 12 hours is typically restricted postoperatively and is not informative for renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. The mean over this window is especially nonspecific.

Average diet status during 13–24h may reflect recovery trajectory (tolerating PO vs not), indirectly associated with AKI through illness severity. As a mean aggregation, it is a blunt proxy and not kidney-specific.

Sustained ability/inability to take diet by 25–36h can reflect overall stabilization versus ongoing critical illness, indirectly linked to AKI risk. Mean aggregation remains nonspecific compared with direct hemodynamic/renal markers.
Any initiation of diet in 0–12h may indicate rapid recovery and lower severity, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. In this early epoch it is common to be NPO, so prognostic value is limited.

Starting diet at any point in 13–24h can indicate improving trajectory and less ongoing shock/ventilation, indirectly lowering AKI risk. It is still a proxy for recovery rather than a renal mechanism.

Ability to tolerate diet by 25–36h is a stronger proxy for clinical stability and fewer ongoing organ-support needs, which correlates with lower AKI risk. It remains indirect and confounded, so moderate importance only.
Minimum diet status in 0–12h largely reflects routine postoperative NPO practice and adds no kidney-specific information. Also redundant with other diet/NPO indicators.

Persistently minimal diet status in 13–24h may reflect delayed recovery and higher acuity, indirectly associated with AKI. As a minimum aggregation it is still nonspecific and overlaps with NPO indicators.

Persistently minimal diet status in 25–36h can indicate ongoing critical illness or procedures, indirectly increasing AKI risk via severity of illness. It is not a direct biomarker and is partially redundant with NPO features.
Measurement frequency of diet orders is a care-process marker and has no direct physiologic link to renal injury; early post-op ordering is routine.
Diet order frequency mainly reflects workflow/acuity rather than kidney perfusion or nephrotoxicity, so it is not a meaningful AKI predictor.
Even close to the outcome window, diet order frequency is not a renal biomarker and is unlikely to add signal beyond illness severity proxies.
Diet type in the immediate post-op period is usually constrained by intubation/NPO status and is not mechanistically tied to AKI; any association would be indirect via overall severity.
Nonstandard diet categories may reflect feeding tolerance or care plans, but have only a weak, indirect relationship to renal outcomes.
By 25–36h, inability to advance diet could correlate with ongoing critical illness, but it remains an indirect proxy rather than a driver of AKI.
Presence of an 'other' diet category early post-op is nonspecific and largely reflects routine NPO/ICU practices rather than renal physiology (and extremes add little here).
A binary indicator of 'other' diet is a weak severity proxy at best and not a recognized AKI risk factor.
Persistent 'other' diet status closer to hour 60 may track ongoing instability, but remains a nonspecific surrogate with limited renal specificity.
The minimum of a binary diet-category indicator is not clinically interpretable for AKI risk and is redundant with other encodings.
Binary-min aggregation provides no meaningful physiologic information and is unlikely to predict creatinine rise.
Binary-min is not a clinically relevant summary and adds collinear noise rather than AKI signal.
How often 'other diet' is documented reflects charting behavior and routine post-op management, not kidney injury mechanisms.
Measurement frequency is a weak behavioral proxy and not a direct predictor of postoperative creatinine trajectory.
Even in the leading-indicator window, documentation frequency of diet category is not a renal biomarker and should carry minimal weight.

Early rhythm regularity can indirectly reflect arrhythmias/low cardiac output that impair renal perfusion, but in 0–12h it is often transient and less prognostic (and mean regularity smooths extremes).

Sustained regular rhythm may reflect hemodynamic stability, but it is an indirect proxy for renal perfusion and not a primary AKI driver. Mean regularity is also less informative than presence of irregularity/arrhythmia extremes.

Closer to the creatinine outcome, persistent regular rhythm suggests stability, but it remains an indirect marker and not kidney-specific. Mean regularity is less pathologic-signal-rich than detecting irregular rhythm episodes.
Early post-op regular rhythm is common and transiently influenced by anesthesia/ICU management, with limited specificity for AKI. A binary max of “regular” adds little beyond absence of arrhythmia.
Having any regular rhythm during the window is expected and not strongly discriminative for AKI risk. This binary maximum is redundant with more informative arrhythmia indicators.
Later regular rhythm may indicate recovery, but a binary “ever regular” is not a strong predictor of renal injury. It is less informative than measures capturing irregular rhythm burden.

If the minimum indicates the patient was continuously regular, that may suggest stability, but in the immediate post-op period this is weakly linked to AKI. Still indirect and non-specific compared with hypotension/vasopressor needs.
Continuous regular rhythm through 13–24h can reflect fewer arrhythmic/hemodynamic insults, which can indirectly reduce AKI risk. However it remains a proxy and not a direct renal perfusion measure.

By 25–36h, persistent regular rhythm may better reflect sustained hemodynamic stability, which supports renal perfusion. Still indirect and should not outrank direct hemodynamic or renal lab features.

Measurement frequency reflects monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. In early post-op care, frequent rhythm documentation is routine and not specific to impending renal injury.
This is primarily a behavioral/acuity proxy and not a direct driver of AKI. Rhythm monitoring frequency is common in ICU patients and poorly specific for renal outcomes.
More frequent rhythm assessment may reflect ongoing instability/concern, which can correlate with AKI risk, but remains indirect. As a _measured feature it should stay low-importance.

Early irregular rhythm (e.g., AF) can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion, but immediate post-op arrhythmias are often transient. Mean irregularity smooths brief episodes, so it is less informative than extremes.

Persistent irregular rhythm in the trajectory phase can indicate ongoing hemodynamic stress and higher AKI risk via reduced forward flow and congestion. Mean aggregation still dilutes episodic severe arrhythmia effects.

Ongoing irregular rhythm close to the outcome window can meaningfully impair renal perfusion and is associated with worse post-cardiac surgery outcomes. Mean irregularity is moderately predictive but less than capturing severe episodes.

Any occurrence of irregular rhythm early post-op can signal arrhythmic insult that may compromise renal perfusion, but this period is confounded by transient post-bypass effects. Binary max captures presence but not burden/severity.

Any irregular rhythm during 13–24h suggests failure to stabilize and can contribute to renal hypoperfusion or venous congestion, increasing AKI risk. As an extreme/presence indicator it is more informative than the mean in this epoch.

Sustained/episodic arrhythmia late (25–36h) can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion, increasing AKI risk. As a binary extreme indicator it can capture clinically meaningful instability, though it is not a direct renal biomarker.

Early postoperative arrhythmias are common and often transient after cardiac surgery, so prognostic value for later creatinine rise is limited. Binary minimum adds little beyond presence/absence and is less informative than later-epoch instability.
Arrhythmia persisting into 13–24h suggests incomplete stabilization and potential ongoing low-flow states affecting kidney perfusion. Still indirect and capped by epoch proximity.

Irregular rhythm close to the outcome window can reflect ongoing hemodynamic compromise (e.g., AF with RVR, ectopy) that predisposes to renal hypoperfusion. As a binary minimum it is moderately informative but not definitive.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/acuity rather than a physiologic mechanism for AKI. In the immediate 0–12h period, frequent documentation is routine after cardiac surgery.
More frequent recording likely reflects clinician concern or instability but is not a direct driver of renal injury. As a behavioral proxy it should remain low importance.

Increased measurement frequency late may indicate ongoing instability/concern (e.g., recurrent arrhythmia) and could weakly correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable rather than a physiologic exposure.
“Other” rhythm categories are heterogeneous and early postoperative rhythm changes are often transient. Mean aggregation further dilutes acute episodes, limiting AKI-specific signal.

A non-standard rhythm classification in 13–24h may reflect ongoing cardiac instability, but the category is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion. Mean/summary coding provides only an indirect link to AKI.

Late persistence of an atypical rhythm classification may correlate with hemodynamic instability closer to the creatinine outcome. However, the “other” label is nonspecific and mean aggregation reduces interpretability.

An extreme occurrence of an atypical rhythm early could indicate a brief instability episode, but 0–12h events are common post-op and often resolve. Max is preferred over mean here, but epoch caps importance.
An extreme episode of an atypical rhythm in 13–24h suggests ongoing instability that can impair renal perfusion. Still indirect and limited by nonspecific rhythm categorization.

A late extreme episode of an atypical rhythm may capture clinically significant instability near the outcome window, plausibly contributing to renal hypoperfusion. Max aggregation best captures acute insults compared with mean.

Minimum/absence-style binary coding of “other” rhythm in the immediate post-op window is not specific and adds limited physiologic information. Early rhythm variability is common and often not predictive of later AKI.

Presence of an atypical rhythm classification into 13–24h may indicate incomplete stabilization, with a plausible indirect link to AKI via low-flow states. As a minimum/binary summary it is less informative than max episodes.

Late persistence of an atypical rhythm classification can reflect ongoing hemodynamic issues that increase AKI risk. As a binary/minimum summary it is moderately predictive but less specific than capturing extreme episodes.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern/monitoring rather than a direct renal mechanism. Early post-op rhythm irregularity documentation is common and nonspecific for AKI.
Still primarily a behavioral proxy for acuity rather than a physiologic driver of kidney injury. May correlate with instability/arrhythmias but is indirect.
More frequent documentation late may indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability/arrhythmias that can reduce renal perfusion, but it remains an indirect proxy measure.

Average HR in the immediate post-op window is often influenced by pain, pacing, and catecholamines and is not specific for renal hypoperfusion. Extremes would better capture shock/tachyarrhythmia burden.
Sustained tachycardia/bradycardia can reflect ongoing low output or arrhythmia risk, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Mean still smooths clinically important extremes.

Persistent abnormal HR closer to outcome may reflect continued low cardiac output or sepsis physiology, which can contribute to AKI. Mean is less informative than min/max for acute insults, so capped for collinearity.
Marked tachycardia can signal bleeding, pain, or low cardiac output states that reduce renal perfusion, but early post-op tachycardia is common and transient (score capped by epoch).
High HR in this trajectory phase more often reflects persistent stress, arrhythmia, or low output requiring support, which increases AKI risk via renal hypoperfusion.
Late sustained or episodic tachycardia is a stronger marker of ongoing hemodynamic compromise/arrhythmia burden near the creatinine outcome window, increasing AKI risk.
Bradycardia can indicate conduction issues, excessive sedation, or pacing problems leading to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op bradycardia can be transient (epoch cap).
Low HR in this phase may better reflect clinically significant low output or conduction disease requiring intervention, which can impair renal perfusion and raise AKI risk.
Late bradycardia near 60h outcome can be a strong indicator of ongoing low cardiac output/pacing dependence with reduced renal blood flow, increasing AKI risk.
Heart rate is continuously monitored in ICU; measurement count mainly reflects documentation frequency rather than physiology. Not a specific predictor of AKI.
Ongoing measurement frequency is routine ICU practice and provides little incremental information about renal injury risk.
Still largely documentation/monitoring behavior rather than a mechanistic or biomarker signal for AKI.
Apical mean HR in the immediate post-op period is nonspecific and influenced by sedation, pacing, and catecholamines. Extremes would better capture hemodynamic insults relevant to AKI.
Sustained tachycardia can reflect ongoing stress/low cardiac output that may reduce renal perfusion, but mean HR is a nonspecific marker and less informative than extremes.
Persistently abnormal HR closer to the outcome can indicate unresolved shock, arrhythmia burden, or inadequate cardiac output affecting kidney perfusion, though it remains indirect.
Early postoperative peak tachycardia may signal bleeding, pain, or low output, but is common/transient immediately after surgery and not specific for AKI (0–12h cap applied).
A high HR extreme in the trajectory phase can indicate ongoing hemodynamic stress (e.g., low output, sepsis, AF with RVR) that increases AKI risk via renal hypoperfusion.
Late-window extreme tachycardia is a stronger leading indicator of persistent instability/low effective perfusion and is more predictive of subsequent creatinine rise.
Early bradycardia extremes may reflect conduction issues or over-sedation and can reduce cardiac output, but immediate post-op brady episodes are often transient (0–12h cap applied).
Bradycardia extremes can imply low cardiac output states or pacing/conduction problems that compromise renal perfusion, making AKI more likely.
Late-window bradycardia extremes suggest persistent low output or significant conduction disease/pacing dependence, which can directly impair renal perfusion and predict AKI.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity/workflow rather than renal physiology; in the immediate post-op period it is routine.
How often HR is documented is largely a care-process variable and not a direct mechanism for AKI.
More frequent HR checks may proxy higher acuity/instability, but it remains an indirect behavioral signal rather than a physiologic driver of AKI.
Mean peripheral HR trends can reflect overall stress/low output, but averages are nonspecific and early post-op derangements are common (0–12h cap applied).
Peripheral mean HR is an indirect marker of hemodynamic stress; to reduce collinearity with more informative extremes, the mean is down-weighted.
Even close to outcome, mean HR is less pathophysiologically specific than max/min extremes for capturing acute instability, so it is capped to avoid redundancy.
Early peripheral HR spikes may indicate pain, hypovolemia, bleeding, or arrhythmia, but are frequent immediately post-op and not specific for AKI (0–12h cap applied).
A high peripheral HR extreme in 13–24h can reflect ongoing low effective perfusion or arrhythmia burden, both of which increase risk for subsequent creatinine rise.
Peripheral HR source mainly reflects monitoring modality (e.g., poor arterial line signal) rather than renal physiology. At most it weakly proxies acuity or perfusion concerns.
In the immediate 0–12h post-op period, use of a peripheral HR source is common and not mechanistically linked to AKI. It is primarily a documentation/monitoring artifact.
Choice of peripheral HR source is a weak proxy for monitoring intensity or line availability, not kidney injury. Limited indirect association with instability at best.
Late reliance on peripheral HR source may reflect line removal or signal issues rather than hemodynamic collapse. Only a weak, indirect link to AKI risk.
Measurement frequency is a behavioral/monitoring proxy and not a physiologic driver of AKI. In 0–12h it is largely routine post-cardiac surgery monitoring.
More frequent peripheral HR documentation may reflect nursing/clinical concern, but it is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion. As a _measured feature it should remain low.
Increased measurement frequency can indicate ongoing instability or closer surveillance, which may correlate with AKI risk but is not causal. As a behavioral proxy it remains low importance.

Need for mechanical ventilation early post-op is common and often transient; it indirectly correlates with illness severity and hemodynamic stress. Mean over 0–12h smooths extremes and is less specific for AKI triggers.

Persistent mechanical ventilation into 13–24h suggests slower recovery, possible shock/low cardiac output, and higher AKI risk via renal hypoperfusion. Mean ventilation status is still an indirect severity marker.

Ongoing mechanical ventilation at 25–36h is a strong marker of continued critical illness (e.g., low output, vasopressor need, fluid overload), which is associated with AKI. It remains indirect but closer in time to the creatinine outcome.

Any mechanical ventilation in 0–12h is expected after cardiac surgery and is not specific for AKI. As an extreme indicator it captures exposure but in this epoch has limited discriminatory value (capped by early-window conservatism).

Mechanical ventilation at any point in 13–24h indicates delayed extubation and higher severity, which correlates with renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Max is more informative than mean for capturing failure to wean.

Mechanical ventilation present in the 25–36h window is a strong leading indicator of ongoing organ dysfunction and hemodynamic/respiratory failure, both linked to AKI. Max best captures the clinically meaningful exposure (still ventilated at some point).

Minimum mechanical ventilation status in 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and often reflects brief periods off the ventilator during routine transitions. It is not a direct AKI driver and is less informative than max.

A minimum indicating time off the ventilator may reflect partial recovery, but it is less predictive than whether ventilation was needed at all. To reduce collinearity, min is kept low relative to max.

Minimum ventilation status in 25–36h mainly indicates whether the patient had any off-vent time, which is less clinically salient than continued need for ventilation. Kept low to avoid redundancy with the more informative max/mean features.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity/ventilation monitoring rather than a renal mechanism; early post-op ventilation is common after cardiac surgery. Weak proxy for severity only.
More frequent documentation/orders may reflect ongoing respiratory/hemodynamic instability, but remains a behavioral proxy. Limited direct linkage to AKI.
Persistent need for mechanical ventilation late suggests ongoing critical illness and possible low cardiac output/inflammation that can contribute to AKI. Still primarily a proxy, so capped low.
